meta.title: Farmer Drop — Propagation (Bass diffusion) working parameters
meta.purpose: Single source of truth for propagation.html — a discrete monthly Bass-diffusion growth model over a bounded church network. A hypothesis generator for the founders' critique whiteboard, NOT a forecast.
meta.open_question: WHICH NETWORK is 'the 12,000 churches'? The two ~12k candidates (Assemblies of God 12,651, semi-centralized districts) and (Churches of Christ 11,776, fully autonomous) imply OPPOSITE go-to-market motions: seed districts top-down vs pure one-church-at-a-time peer contagion. Unresolved — governs the whole GTM and the choice of q. Founders (Volkmar, Magnus) must decide.
assumptions_flagged: Every church modeled at the SAME median size (70) — but ~50% of churchgoers sit in the largest ~10% of churches (Lifeway 2025); WHICH churches adopt matters as much as how many.
A single q for the whole network — an autonomous (CoC) and a district (AoG) network do not share a contagion rate.
Participation holds flat after year 1 with zero church/host churn (optimistic).
Church basket $35 differs from the main-graph $100 scale basket — the scale story mixes two basket assumptions.

Growth speed (Bass) and survival are DIFFERENT axes: every precedent died at the CENTER (fraud / subsidy withdrawal / competitive erosion), never in the church layer. Governance & funding integrity is an independent failure variable NOT captured by this model.

model.note: Discrete monthly update: N[t+1] = min(M, N[t] + (p + q*N[t]/M)*(M - N[t])). users(t) = N(t) * attendance * householdsPerAttendee * participation(t). weeklyGross = users(t) * basket. Months indexed 1..96.

targets.note: Marker lines on the chart. From farmer_drop_graph.json scale_scenario: year-1 target 100k users, scale 1M users.

ceilings.label: CoC · Texas
ceilings.note: 1,901 Churches of Christ in Texas (most of any state) — autonomous beachhead

scenarios.name: Conservative
scenarios.justification: Autonomous-network pure contagion (CoC-TX): no top-down channel, q halved; half-saturation 6–8+ yrs. Peaks ~2k weekly users in 8 yr — never approaches 100k.

scenarios.name: Base
scenarios.justification: Canonical Bass + light promotion across a metro-scale set (~DFW); half-saturation ~4–6 yrs. Peaks ~14k weekly users in 8 yr.

scenarios.name: Angel-Food-speed
scenarios.justification: Requires a funded central warehouse/logistics backbone — the 2005 $6.9M USDA loan is what unlocked Angel Food's hockey stick. Peaks ~69k weekly users in 8 yr on median-70 churches — still under 100k without big-church weighting.

computed_readouts_at_defaults.note: months-to-threshold as computed by the page's own model (cross-checked in Python). At median attendance 70 all three scenarios SATURATE below the targets — that is the load-bearing finding.

reachability.comment: 1M users at median-70 churches, 12% participation needs ~276,855 churches — ~6x the entire SBC (46,876). 1M is an addressable-market claim, not a diffusion-speed claim.
reachability.angel_food_speed_reaches_100k_if: attendance raised toward the attendee-weighted mean (~360): reaches 100k around month 37 — still NOT within year 1. Year-1 100k requires recruiting big (250+) churches from launch plus high q + high participation.

networks.name: Churches of Christ
networks.motion: One church at a time (peer contagion)

congregation_size.source: NCS-IV / Faith Communities Today (FACT) / Lifeway Research 2025
congregation_size.implication: Recruiting a few 250+ churches captures outsized demand; the median-70 slider understates a big-church strategy and overstates a random-church one.

precedents.name: Angel Food Ministries
precedents.inflection: 2005 $6.9M USDA warehouse loan unlocked logistics backbone
precedents.lesson: The hockey stick came from central logistics capital, not more churches; the network never failed — the CENTER did.

precedents.name: USDA Farmers-to-Families Food Box
precedents.lesson: A subsidy-dependent center is a policy risk.

precedents.name: SHARE Food Network
precedents.outcome: eroded competitively; survived by institutionalizing as a nonprofit food hub
precedents.lesson: Volunteer buying clubs erode as grocery/online discounting catches up unless institutionalized.

sources.label: Assemblies of God — 12,651 churches (2024)
sources.note: graphsaboutreligion.com; semi-centralized district polity → real top-down channel.

sources.label: Churches of Christ — 11,776 (2022); 1,901 in Texas
sources.note: 2022 directory; fully autonomous congregations → one-church-at-a-time contagion; Texas has the most of any state.

sources.label: Congregation size — NCS-IV / FACT / Lifeway 2025
sources.note: Median weekly attendance ~70 (COVID trough ~45); attendee-weighted mean ~360; ~50% of churchgoers in the largest ~10% of churches.

sources.label: Angel Food Ministries
sources.note: 1994 (34 families) → ~5,000 host churches, 500k families/mo, 43+ states, $137.5M revenue (2008) after a 2005 $6.9M USDA warehouse loan; collapsed 2011 on a 49-count federal fraud indictment.

sources.label: USDA Farmers-to-Families Food Box
sources.note: ~176M boxes via faith & nonprofit partners; ended by policy, not demand.

sources.label: SHARE Food Network
sources.note: ~300 church host sites, ~12,000 families/month (DC); eroded competitively, survived by institutionalizing.

sources.label: Bass diffusion & analog growth rates
sources.note: External p ≈ 0.03/yr, peer q ≈ 0.35–0.40/yr; Celebrate Recovery ~43%/yr for decades; per-contact adoption ~15% (10–25%); half-saturation ~6–8 yrs organic, ~3–4 promoted.
